Learning the Policy. We use the received reward rt for
an action zt to update the policy π using the gradient bandit     Table 1: Four victim blackboxes FV . Each blackbox is named

algorithm [43]. This update is equivalent to a backward-
pass through the tree (denoted by a green line in Figure 4b),

                                                                                                cess victim’s test data. Hence, we create test sets for each

formance? We study the influence using two choices of                                           tion of images are available). We present each variant using

nificantly more complex Resnet-101/Densenet-161 are at                                          role: both Resnet-34 and Resnet-101 show similar perfor-
the other; (ii) performance transfers across model fami-                                        mance indicating a compact architecture is sufficient to cap-

more complex model architecture of the knockoff is bene-                                        the real-world with knockoffs exhibiting a strong perfor-
ficial. This contrasts KD settings where the objective is to                                    mance. The knockoff circumvents monetary and labour
have a more compact student (knockoff) model.                                                   costs of: (a) collecting images for the task; (b) obtaining
                                                                                                expert annotations; and (c) tuning a model. As a result, an
6.3. Stealing Functionality of a Real-world Black-                                              inexpensive knockoff is trained which exhibits strong per-
      box Model                                                                                 formance, using victim API queries amounting to only $30.
    Now we validate model functionality stealing on a pop-
ular image analysis API. Such image recognition services                                        7. Conclusion
are gaining popularity allowing users to obtain image-
predictions for a variety of tasks at low costs ($1-2 per 1k                                       We investigated the problem of model functionality
queries). These image recognition APIs have also been                                           stealing where an adversary transfers the functionality of a
used to evaluate other attacks e.g., adversarial examples                                       victim model into a knockoff via blackbox access. In spite
[4, 18, 23]. We focus on a facial characteristics API which                                     of minimal assumptions on the blackbox, we demonstrated
given an image, returns attributes and confidences per face.                                    the surprising effectiveness of our approach. Finally, we
Note that in this experiment, we have semantic information                                      validated our approach on a popular image recognition API
of blackbox output classes.                                                                     and found strong performance of knockoffs. We find func-
Collecting PA . The API returns probability vectors per                                         tionality stealing poses a real-world threat that potentially
face in the image and thus, querying irrelevant images leads                                    undercuts an increasing number of deployed ML models.
to a wasted result with no output information. Hence, we                                        Acknowledgement. This research was partially supported
use two face image sets PA for this experiment: CelebA                                          by the German Research Foundation (DFG CRC 1223). We
(220k images) [24] and OpenImages-Faces (98k images).                                           thank Yang Zhang for helpful discussions.

 D. Auxiliary Experiments                                                                      cating exploration goals (diversity, loss) are necessary when
    In this section, we present experiments to supplement                                      transitioning to an open-world scenario.
 existing results in the main paper.
 D.1. Seen and Unseen classes
     We now discuss evaluation to supplement Section 5.2.1
 and Section 6.
     In 6.1 we highlighted strong performance of the knock-
 off even among classes that were never encountered (see Ta-
 ble 3 for exact numbers) during training. To elaborate, we
 split the blackbox output classes into “seen” and “unseen”
 categories and present mean per-class accuracies in Figure
 19. Although we find better performance on classes seen
 while training the knockoff, performance of unseen classes
 is remarkably high, with the knockoff achieving >70% per-
 formance in both cases.
 D.2. Adaptive: With and without hierarchy
     The adaptive strategy presented in Section 4.1.2 uses
 a hierarchy discussed in Section 5.2.2. As a result, we ap-
 proached this as a hierarchical multi-armed bandit problem.
 Now, we present an alternate approach adaptive-flat,
 without the hierarchy. This is simply a multi-armed bandit
 problem with |Z| arms (actions).
     Figure 20 illustrates the performance of these approaches
 using PA = D2 (|Z| = 2129) and rewards {certainty, diver-
 sity, loss}. We observe adaptive consistently outperforms
